title: Hypoxic-Ischemic Encephalopathy
authors: Groenendaal F, de Vries LS / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Hypoxic-Ischemic Encephalopathy?
HIE เป็น emergency ที่มี therapeutic window แคบที่สุดในนีโอนาทอโลจี — 6 ชั่วโมงหลังคลอด คือเวลาทั้งหมดที่เรามีในการป้องกัน secondary energy failure. (1) Incidence: 1–2/1000 live births ในประเทศพัฒนาแล้ว; สูงกว่ามากในประเทศกำลังพัฒนา — ยังเป็น leading cause ของ neonatal death + CP; (2) Therapeutic hypothermia เป็น standard of care ตั้งแต่ 2005 ลดผลลัพธ์เลวร้าย (death หรือ severe disability) จาก ~65% → ~45%; NNT ~7; (3) Secondary energy failure เริ่มใน 6–8 ชั่วโมง — ถ้าเริ่ม cooling หลัง 6h ประสิทธิภาพลดลงอย่างมีนัยสำคัญ (Laptook JAMA 2017); (4) EPO ไม่ช่วยเมื่อใช้ร่วมกับ hypothermia (Wu NEJM 2022;387:148) — ต้องอัพเดทความรู้; (5) Mild HIE ไม่ใช่ benign — ADHD, perceptual-motor difficulties, need for special education พบได้สูง; school-age outcomes แย่กว่า term controls แม้จะไม่มี CP.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
ให้ definition ที่ถูกต้องของ HIE, perinatal asphyxia, hypoxia, ischemia, neonatal encephalopathy และอธิบายว่าทำไม ACOG แนะนำ 'neonatal encephalopathy' แทน 'HIE'
อธิบาย two-phase injury: primary energy failure + secondary energy failure (timeline, mechanism, therapeutic window)
อธิบาย glutamate excitotoxicity → Ca²⁺ influx → nNOS → peroxynitrite → mitochondrial failure → apoptosis/necrosis
จำแนก Sarnat stages I–III และ Thompson score: clinical features, items, วิธีใช้ในการ select สำหรับ hypothermia
อธิบาย selective vulnerability: term (basal ganglia/thalamus ใน acute total; watershed/parasagittal ใน prolonged partial) vs preterm (PVL, preoligodendrocytes)
อธิบาย neuropathology: selective neuronal necrosis, parasagittal injury, BGT injury, PVL, status marmoratus, ulegyria
อธิบาย EEG/aEEG: background patterns (normal, moderately abnormal, severely abnormal/burst suppression, isoelectric), seizure detection limitations, sleep-wake cycling
อธิบาย MRI in HIE: DWI findings (ADC pseudonormalization), 1H-MRS (lactate, NAA), patterns (BGT vs watershed), timing ของ MRI
อธิบาย multiorgan involvement: cardiac, hepatic, hematologic (DIC) — กี่ % และ management
บอก protocol ของ therapeutic hypothermia: เกณฑ์ inclusion, target temp, duration, complications (bradycardia, thrombocytopenia), evidence base (8 RCTs, meta-analysis)
จัดการ seizures ใน HIE: phenobarbital first line, lidocaine add-on (ไม่ใช่ถ้าให้ phenytoin แล้ว), levetiracetam inferior; pharmakokinetics เปลี่ยนระหว่าง cooling
ระบุ prognostic factors: Apgar scores, cord gas pH, encephalopathy severity, MRI pattern, aEEG background, evoked potentials, neurobiomarkers
1. Definitions และ Incidence
1.1 Key Definitions

rows: Abnormal neurologic behavior in neonatal period following perinatal hypoxia-ischemia
Preferred term (ACOG 2014): etiology not always obvious; clinical syndrome without implying cause
Interrupted supply of O₂ through placenta/umbilical cord to fetus → combined hypoxemia + hypercapnia
Partial (hypoxia) or complete (anoxia) lack of O₂ supply to brain or blood; hypoxemia = lack of O₂ in blood
Reduction or cessation of blood flow to organ → compromise of both O₂ AND substrate (glucose) delivery
Acute total hypoxic-ischemic insult (e.g., cord prolapse, uterine rupture) → basal ganglia/brainstem injury pattern

blocks.text: 1.2 Epidemiology
Incidence: 1–2 per 1000 live births in Western world; significantly higher in developing countries (see Chapter 7)
Risk with severe perinatal asphyxia: up to 65% death or irreversible damage without treatment
With therapeutic hypothermia: adverse outcome reduced to ~45%; NNT ~7 (number needed to treat to prevent one death or severe disability)

Preterm brain: more resistant to hypoxia than term brain — lower cerebral metabolic rate; lower sensitivity to neurotransmitter neurotoxicity; greater plasticity — BUT neuropathology different (see Ch 54)
2. Pathophysiology — Two-Phase Injury
2.1 Physiologic Response to Hypoxia-Ischemia

With onset of hypoxemia: (1) fetal bradycardia + ↑ blood pressure → redistribution of cardiac output → ↑ cerebral + vital organ perfusion at expense of periphery; (2) ongoing hypoxemia → ↑ bradycardia, apnea; (3) permanent brain injury after 10–15 min
Fetus resistant to mild hypoxemia: normal cardiovascular function maintained up to 1 hour at PaO₂ 15 mmHg (normal fetal PaO₂ = 25 mmHg)
Brainstem vs cerebrum: brainstem extracts sufficient O₂ even at very low PaO₂ at expense of cerebrum; failing myocardium → ↓ cardiac output → watershed areas most exposed

Acute 'sentinel event' (cord prolapse, uterine rupture): → basal ganglia + brainstem injury; prolonged partial insult: → cerebral hemisphere damage, watershed distribution (Myers RE Am J Obstet Gynecol 1972)
2.2 Primary and Secondary Energy Failure

Two-Phase Injury — Therapeutic Window
Phase 1 — Primary energy failure: failure of Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase → Na⁺ + Cl⁻ influx → cytotoxic edema; anaerobic glycolysis → lactic acidosis; free radical production initiated; primary necrosis if not reversed. Latent period (30–60 min after resuscitation): apparent recovery of cerebral oxidative metabolism; cytotoxic edema and excitatory amino acids resolve temporarily. Phase 2 — Secondary energy failure: begins 6–8 hours after primary insult; mitochondrial failure; ↓ ATP + PCr shown by 31P-MRS (Hope Lancet 1984, Lorek Pediatr Res 1994); lasts up to 72 hours. Therapeutic window = 6 hours — this is the window before secondary energy failure to start hypothermia. Starting hypothermia later than 6h is far less effective (Laptook JAMA 2017;318:1550).
2.3 Excitotoxicity Cascade
Glutamate toxicity: ↑ extracellular glutamate → NMDA receptor activation → unregulated Ca²⁺ influx

Ca²⁺ influx activates: lipases, proteases, endonucleases, phospholipase C → organelle membrane breakdown → free radicals (NO•, superoxide) → mitochondrial failure → caspase-3 → DNA fragmentation + apoptosis

Nitric oxide: nNOS + iNOS → NO• neurotoxic at high concentrations; NO• + O₂•⁻ → peroxynitrite → lipid peroxidation; up to 80% of NMDA toxicity mediated through NO•; eNOS (endothelial) essential for maintaining cerebral perfusion — only nNOS/iNOS-specific inhibitors usable for neuroprotection
Free iron: higher in immature brain (low transferrin); catalyzes Fenton reaction: O₂•⁻ → ↑ toxic free radicals; ↑ intraneuronal free iron within 24h of HI insult

Apoptosis vs necrosis: continuum depending on tissue O₂ content; apoptosis = Bcl-2/Bax/APAF1/caspase family; sex difference: XY → AIF-dependent pathway; XX → cytochrome c-dependent → caspase-3 pathway (Renolleau 2008) — EPO more protective in female pups; relevance in humans unestablished

Cytokines: TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-18 + caspase-1 activated in microglia after HI → neuroinflammation without infection; chorioamnionitis significantly exacerbates clinical + neuronal injury (Shi Pediatrics 2017)
2.4 Preconditioning

Prior nondamaging hypoxic events → reduced brain sensitivity to subsequent injury through HIF-1α stabilization during hypoxia → dimerizes with HIF-1β → activates hypoxia-response genes → erythropoiesis, angiopoiesis, antiapoptosis
Preconditioning in rat pups: exposure to moderate hypoxia or inflammation before HI → neuroprotective (Vexler Cond Med 2018)
3. Selective Vulnerability และ Neuropathology
3.1 Factors Influencing Injury Pattern
GA <20 wk insult: during neuronal migration → heterotopia or polymicrogyria
Mid-gestation (26–36 wk): predominant WM injury → cystic PVL; secondary effects on deep gray matter growth; ↑ IVH risk
Near/at term (≥35 wk): deep gray matter (BGT) injury OR watershed/parasagittal injury — pattern depends on type of insult
3.2 Two Patterns of Term HIE

rows: Cord prolapse, uterine rupture, acute abruption
Basal ganglia + thalamus (BGT) + brainstem + spinal cord
BGT signal abnormality; posterior limb PLIC signal loss
Parasagittal cortical injury, watershed DWI restriction
Spastic quadriplegia or diplegia; cognitive impairment
Chronic villitis → BGT pattern (Chalak J Pediatr 2021)

blocks.text: 3.3 Watershed Areas
Term brain cortical watershed: parasagittal region between three main arteries supplying each hemisphere → most vulnerable to ↓ cerebral perfusion
Sulcal depths: watershed areas at term → columnar necrosis with ↓ sulcal perfusion → ulegyria in older child (mushroom gyri — loss of deep sulcal GM)
Volpe PET study (Ann Neurol 1985;17:287): consistent ↓ CBF to parasagittal regions in 17 full-term asphyxiated newborns
3.4 Neuropathology Patterns
Selective neuronal necrosis: most common; CA1 hippocampus, cerebral cortex layers III/V/VI, deep cerebellar nuclei, inferior olivary nuclei, dorsal horn; patterns vary with severity
Parasagittal cerebral injury: cortical + subcortical WM necrosis in parasagittal distribution; bilateral, often asymmetric; associated with chronic partial asphyxia
BGT/thalamic injury: posterior + lateral thalamic nuclei + lentiform nuclei; ↑ regional glucose metabolism + glutamate AMPA receptors explain selective vulnerability
Status marmoratus: develops ~6 months post-insult; patchy marble-like appearance of basal ganglia + thalamus; histology = abnormal myelination of glial bundles (not neurons)
Ulegyria: chronic phase of sulcal watershed injury; mushroom-like gyri from loss of deep GM in sulci

Cerebral edema: frequent in severe HI; visible on imaging after 24h (high water content of neonatal brain masks it earlier); brain swelling + slit-like ventricles → mainly with 'near-total' injury pattern
Cerebellar injury: underestimated with MRI vs histology (Annink Pediatr Res 2021;89:1171); affects cerebellar hemisphere + deep cerebellar nuclei
4. Clinical Assessment — Grading Systems
4.1 Sarnat Classification (Modified)
Modified Sarnat used within 6-hour window for hypothermia eligibility decision (Chalak Pediatr Res 2018;84:861)

Predictive value: mild HIE → virtually no risk major ND disability; moderate HIE → 75% survive without major deficits (pre-hypothermia era); severe HIE → 50–100% mortality + 65–75% severe disability in survivors
With hypothermia: severity now primarily a selection criterion rather than outcome predictor — hypothermia improves outcome, especially in moderate HIE
4.2 Thompson Score (Table 56.2)
9 items (tone, consciousness, seizures, posture, Moro, grasp, suck, respiration, fontanelle); each scored 0–3
Serial daily scoring: Day 1, Day 2, Day 3; ↑ predictive with serial assessment
IPPV requirement added as separate item — total score per day correlates with neurodevelopmental outcome at 12 months (Thompson Acta Paediatr 1997;86:757)

Hypothermia Eligibility — Standard Criteria
GA ≥36 weeks; ≤6 hours of age. EITHER: (A) pH <7.00 or base deficit ≥16 mmol/L on cord/postnatal blood gas within 1 hour; OR (B) acute perinatal event (e.g., cord prolapse, abruption, uterine rupture) + either Apgar ≤5 at 10 min or need for resuscitation at 10 min. PLUS: moderate or severe encephalopathy on clinical exam (Sarnat Stage II or III) or aEEG background abnormality (moderate: continuous low-voltage, burst suppression; severe: isoelectric). Mild HIE: evidence for cooling accumulating but not yet confirmed in RCTs — ongoing studies.
5. Neurophysiology และ Neuroimaging
5.1 EEG and aEEG
16-channel EEG: gold standard; 16 channels + video; requires skilled neurophysiologist; ACNS guidelines 2011 (Shellhaas J Clin Neurophysiol 2011;28:611)

Background patterns — prognostic value: (1) Normal/mildly abnormal: → very low risk seizures, almost invariably normal outcome; (2) Moderately abnormal: → prediction less reliable, needs serial recordings; (3) Severely abnormal (burst suppression, isoelectric, extremely low voltage): → strongly predicts adverse outcome; long periods of inactivity + brief bursts (<6s) + interhemispheric asymmetry = especially poor

aEEG: single/two-channel (parietal electrodes); semi-log amplitude compression; continuous monitoring; limitations: misses short, low-amplitude, and focal seizures (sensitivity 27–56% single channel; 76% two-channel with raw EEG); positive predictive value for poor outcome 59% at 3–6h with hypothermia (vs 84% normothermia); normalize by 24h (good outcome) or 48h (good outcome under cooling) (Thoresen Pediatrics 2010;126:e131)
Sleep-wake cycling: onset and quality reflects HIE severity; presence + time of onset predicts NDO; recovery slower during hypothermia

Seizure burden: <10% of neonatal seizures identified by clinical staff vs video-EEG (Murray Arch Dis Child FN 2008;93:F187); electrographic seizures independently associated with ↑ MRI injury (OR 5.00 95% CI 1.47–17.05, Shah Arch Dis Child FN 2014;99:F219)

Long-term epilepsy: 13% develop epilepsy post-cooling; 35% of those → infantile spasms; abnormal NDO in 81% with epilepsy vs 27% without; discontinuation of ASM before discharge appears safe (Glass JAMA Neurol 2021;78:817)
5.2 Evoked Potentials
BAEPs (brainstem auditory evoked potentials): technically easiest; reflect brainstem integrity; hearing impairment in 4–7% after HIE (Fitzgerald 2019;23:81)
VEPs (visual evoked potentials): predict visual outcome; abnormal VEP + abnormal MRI = high specificity for adverse outcome

SEPs (somatosensory evoked potentials): bilateral cortical SEP loss → severe MRI lesions (Suppiej 2018;129:95); strong predictor of motor outcome (Cainelli 2018;129:1300); at 9–10 yr follow-up, abnormal early SEP/VEP = strongest predictor (Kontio 2013;124:1089)
5.3 Neuroimaging
Cranial ultrasound: first-line; detects IVH, major structural abnormalities, basal ganglia echogenicity; less sensitive than MRI for cortical + WM injury

DWI (diffusion-weighted MRI): restricted diffusion = cytotoxic edema in first week; ADC pseudonormalization ~day 7–10 (Bednarek Neurology 2012;78:1420) — cystic areas then show ↑ ADC above normal; optimal timing for DWI = days 2–5 (before pseudonormalization)

Conventional MRI (T1/T2): best performed at 3–7 days postnatal; PLIC signal loss = strong predictor of adverse motor outcome; BGT signal + watershed pattern inform prognosis (Martinez-Biarge Neurology 2011;76:2055)
1H-MRS: lactate in grey matter = poor prognosis (present after 48h in affected infants); ↓ NAA = neuronal loss; Lac/Naa ratio predicts outcome (Barta BMC Pediatr 2018;18:302)

NIRS (near-infrared spectroscopy): ↑ brain O₂ saturation + ↓ O₂ uptake = secondary energy failure (Lemmers Pediatr Res 2013;74:180); PPHN → ↓ brain oxygenation during hypothermia → use NIRS to guide management

MRI score (Weeke J Pediatr 2018;192:33): novel scoring system predicting NDO post-asphyxia + hypothermia; mammillary body atrophy at school age associated with ↓ IQ + ↓ episodic memory (Annink Sci Rep 2021;11:5017)

Origin and timing of injury (Cowan Lancet 2003;361:736): MRI study of term HIE: majority of brain damage occurs in immediate perinatal period; not long-standing damage — DWI absent in long-standing injury
6. Multiorgan Involvement
6.1 Cardiovascular
Myocardial dysfunction: detectable by Doppler echo in many asphyxiated infants; ↓ contractility → ↓ cardiac output → ↓ CBF + ↓ organ perfusion
Complications: acute cardiac dilation + functional tricuspid regurgitation; myocardial ischemia on ECG; ischemic necrosis of papillary muscle; troponin T as biomarker (under investigation)
PPHN: not increased by hypothermia itself; BUT common when perinatal asphyxia + early-onset sepsis or MAS; ↑ impact on brain oxygenation in hypothermia-treated infants → iNO + milrinone
Hypotension: common; volume support often ineffective; inotropes effective in restoring BP in asphyxiated infants
6.2 Hepatic and GI
Liver: ↑ liver enzymes in 80–85% of full-term asphyxiated neonates in first week; irreversible damage rare
NEC: recognized complication of asphyxia but relatively rare as primary cause

Glucose: hyperinsulinism post-asphyxia (Collins Lancet 1984): insulin ↑ in hours after perinatal asphyxia when stress hormones ↓ → ↑ hypoglycemia risk; must avoid hypoglycemia — adds to brain injury (Parmentier J Pediatr 2022;245:30); monitor blood glucose regularly; routine high-concentration glucose not advised; variable glucose levels common post-resuscitation
6.3 Hematologic
Coagulopathy: relatively common; ↑ thrombin-antithrombin complexes, D-dimer, fibrin/fibrinogen degradation products, soluble fibrin monomer complexes
DIC: recognized after birth asphyxia; low factor XIII; management controversial; no indication for systemic heparinization; replace platelets/clotting factors as indicated
Hypothermia effect: does not worsen coagulation overall, but ↓ thrombocyte counts during cooling; anticipate + monitor
7. Therapeutic Hypothermia — Standard of Care
7.1 Evidence Base
8 RCTs in full-term neonates with perinatal asphyxia + encephalopathy published since 2005 (Gunn 1998 → Jacobs 2011)

Cochrane meta-analysis (Jacobs 2013, CD003311): consistent improvement in survival without neurologic abnormality + ↓ death + severe disability; risk difference −0.14 (95% CI −0.19 to −0.09); Z = 5.29, P <0.00001; I² = 29%
NNT ~7: to prevent one death or major handicap; neuroprotection better in moderate vs severe encephalopathy
No difference: between head cooling (CoolCap) and whole-body hypothermia in Cochrane analysis
7.2 Protocol

Therapeutic Hypothermia Protocol
Target temperature: Whole-body: rectal temp 33.0–34.0°C for 72 hours. Head-cooling (CoolCap): coolant at 10°C, body temp 34–35°C. Start within 6 hours of birth: based on secondary energy failure timeline in animal models. Starting later than 6h significantly less effective (Laptook JAMA 2017;318:1550). Rewarming: gradual at 0.5°C/hour after 72 hours. Temperature limits: do NOT cool below 33°C — cardiac arrhythmia risk. GA restriction: ≥35 weeks' gestation — historical data show vast excess of deaths in premature infants when cold; NOT recommended in preterm infants. Mild encephalopathy: therapeutic effects NOT yet confirmed in RCTs; ongoing studies.
7.3 Complications of Hypothermia
Arrhythmia: RR 4.08 (95% CI 1.55–10.74) — sinus bradycardia most common; monitor ECG continuously
Thrombocytopenia: RR 1.28 (95% CI 1.07–1.52); monitor platelet counts
No ↑ risk: coagulopathy, hypotension, renal failure, hepatic side effects, infection, pulmonary hypertension in RCT meta-analysis (Shah Semin Fetal Neonatal Med 2010;15:238)
Anecdotal (not proven in RCTs): ↑ pulmonary hypertension, subcutaneous fat necrosis — monitor clinically
Control infants who became hyperthermic: had worse outcomes than normothermic controls — avoid hyperthermia in all HIE infants
7.4 Long-Term Outcomes After Hypothermia
School-age outcomes (Lee-Kelland Arch Dis Child FN 2020;105:8): children without CP still showed ↓ cognitive + motor performance + more emotional difficulties vs age/sex/SES-matched term controls
Mammillary body atrophy (Annink Sci Rep 2021;11:5017): associated with ↓ total + performance IQ, ↓ processing speed, ↓ episodic memory at school age — reflects hippocampal circuit injury
Smaller hippocampal volumes: also associated with worse cognitive + memory outcomes at school age
8. Seizure Management ใน HIE
8.1 Drug of Choice

Anticonvulsant Algorithm in HIE
First line — Phenobarbital: IV; 20–40 mg/kg loading dose; effective seizure control <50% as monotherapy. Second line — Phenytoin: no greater efficacy than phenobarbital; combined with phenobarbital → only 50% complete abolition. Second/third line — Lidocaine: more effective as add-on therapy after phenobarbital vs midazolam (Weeke Epilepsia 2016;57:233), especially during hypothermia; DO NOT use if phenytoin has been given — cardiotoxicity risk. Levetiracetam vs phenobarbital RCT (Sharpe Pediatrics 2020;145:e20036806): phenobarbital 80% seizure-free vs levetiracetam 28% (P <.001) — levetiracetam INFERIOR as first-line. Pharmacokinetics during hypothermia: pharmacokinetics of lidocaine and midazolam changed by cooling — dose adjustments required. Discontinuation of ASM (Glass JAMA Neurol 2021): safe to discontinue before discharge after acute symptomatic seizures; 2-year NDO similar with or without ASM at home.
8.2 Seizure Burden and Brain Injury
Animal models: neonatal seizures → permanent disruption of neuronal development; seizures superimposed on HI → significantly exacerbate brain injury
Human: high seizure burden = significant risk factor for mortality, LOS, abnormal exam at discharge (Glass J Pediatr 2016;174:98)
Clinical electrographic dissociation: <10% of seizures recognized by clinical staff (Murray 2008); must use continuous EEG/aEEG for accurate diagnosis
Note: several studies show ↓ seizure burden in infants who received cooling — may partially explain therapeutic benefit of hypothermia
9. Other Neuroprotective Strategies
9.1 Adjunct Strategies — Current Status

rows: DOES NOT reduce death or ND impairment when combined with hypothermia (Wu NEJM 2022;387:148) — negative phase III RCT; do NOT add to hypothermia
Antioxidant + antiapoptotic; ↑ pharmacokinetic studies needed; possibly beneficial but inadequate clinical evidence

Xanthine oxidase inhibitor + free-radical scavenger; long-term follow-up at 5 yr: no overall difference; ↓ adverse outcome in moderately asphyxiated subgroup (RR 0.40, 95% CI 0.17–0.94); maternal administration ALBINO trial ongoing (phase III, Maiwald BMC Pediatr 2019)
NMDA antagonist + anesthetic; neuroprotective in animal models; NO clinical benefit demonstrated in combination with hypothermia; limited by excessive cost
NMDA receptor antagonist; no sustained benefits in term asphyxiated infants despite RCTs
Glutathione precursor + antioxidant + free-radical scavenger; combined with hypothermia in animal experiments; no clinical human studies
Preclinical benefit; autologous cord blood infusion trials ongoing (Cotten J Pediatr 2014;164:973)
NOT safe: rapid ↓ MAP + ↓ CBF in 3/4 asphyxiated infants — systemic effects limit CNS selectivity

blocks.text: 10. Prognosis
10.1 Prognostic Factors

BOX 56.3 — Predictors of Mortality and Neurologic Morbidity
• Extended (20 min) very low Apgar scores. • Long time to establish spontaneous respiration (>30 min → universal severe damage in pre-hypothermia era; BUT: 5/13 survivors with 10-min Apgar = 0 were normal at 18–22 months in hypothermia era [Shukla Pediatrics 2022;149] — Apgar predictive value changed). • Prolonged abnormal neonatal neurologic examination. • Abnormal brain imaging (US + MRI + 1H-MRS). • Abnormal electrophysiology: suppressed EEG/aEEG background, status epilepticus, abnormal evoked potentials (VEP, BAEP, SEP).
10.2 Specific Predictors

Cord gas/acidosis: umbilical artery pH <7.00 in 2.5% of 3506 full-term neonates; 66.7% had metabolic component; ↑ neonatal death in severely acidotic group; metabolic acidosis at birth still associated with severe brain injury in hypothermia era (Sabir Semin Fetal Neonatal Med 2015;20:115)

Neurobiomarkers: activin A, GFAP, NSE, S100B, adrenomedullin → biomarkers of brain damage degree/extension (Bersani Clin Chem Lab Med 2020;58:471); prostanoids + non-protein-bound iron = oxidative biomarkers (Tataranno Clin Perinatol 2015;42:529)
aEEG background pattern: positive predictive value 59% at 3–6h with hypothermia (vs 84% normothermia); normalize by 24h = good outcome; normalize by 48h = good outcome under cooling
MRI + 1H-MRS: strongest combined predictor of adverse motor outcome and cognitive outcome (also following introduction of hypothermia); MRI + EEG = basis for most accurate prediction of disability
10.3 Long-Term Neurodisability — 'HIE Is Not Binary'
Major ND problems: only after moderate + severe HIE; death still common after severe HIE

Mild and moderate HIE WITHOUT major deficit: ↓ full-scale IQ; minor neurologic dysfunction; perceptual-motor difficulties; ADHD requiring additional educational support (Conway Early Hum Dev 2018;120:80)
Moderate HIE survivors: ↑ need for special education
School-age in post-hypothermia era: ↓ cognitive + motor + emotional outcomes vs controls even in children without CP (Lee-Kelland 2020)
Assessment of ALL contributing factors recommended: maternal medical history, obstetric antecedents, intrapartum fetal HR monitoring, delivery issues, placental pathology (ACOG Task Force 2014)
11. NICU Practical — HIE Management
11.1 Immediate Assessment
Within 1 hour of birth: cord or early postnatal blood gas;
Clinical neurologic exam: Sarnat staging; Thompson score; aEEG initiation within first hour of life in suspected HIE
Lab screen: BG, CBC + plt, LFTs, coagulation screen (PT/PTT/fibrinogen/D-dimer), troponin, CMP; urine output monitoring
Decision on hypothermia: within 6 hours; document time of perinatal event vs time of hypothermia initiation
11.2 Brain-Oriented Care During Hypothermia
Glucose: check frequently; target normoglycemia (avoid hypoglycemia AND avoid very high glucose); hypoglycemia + HIE → additional brain injury (Parmentier J Pediatr 2022)

Seizures: treat clinical + electrographic; phenobarbital first; consider lidocaine add-on (not after phenytoin); aEEG/cEEG required for detection; adjust anticonvulsant dosing for hypothermia pharmacokinetics
Avoid cerebral edema interventions: no evidence for corticosteroids; no evidence for routine ICP monitoring; raised ICP = end-result marker, not primary target
Cardiac support: monitor for ↓ CO, TR, myocardial ischemia; inotropes if hypotension persists after volume; iNO + milrinone for PPHN
MRI timing: DWI days 2–5 (before ADC pseudonormalization); conventional MRI days 3–7; spectroscopy days 7–14 (NAA most informative)
Prognosis discussion: combine clinical exam + aEEG + MRI + evoked potentials; avoid single-parameter decisions; explain school-age risks even for 'good' outcome infants to family
11.3 Post-Hypothermia Monitoring
Continue aEEG until normothermic and background normalized; assess sleep-wake cycling
Hearing screen: ↑ risk SNHL post-HIE; BAEP before discharge (Smit J Pediatr 2013;163:995)
Ophthalmology: visual outcomes depend on watershed (occipital) vs BGT pattern; VEP before discharge if moderate-severe
Neurodevelopmental follow-up: Griffiths/Bayley at 18–24 months minimum; school-age cognitive + behavioral assessment (Griffiths ND follow-up at 5–8 yr for hippocampal/memory outcomes)
12. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — Therapeutic Window: 6 ชั่วโมง = ทั้งหมด
Secondary energy failure เริ่มใน 6–8 ชั่วโมงหลัง primary insult — ช่วงนี้คือ therapeutic window สำหรับ hypothermia. ถ้าเริ่ม cooling หลัง 6h: ประสิทธิภาพลดลงอย่างมีนัยสำคัญ (Laptook JAMA 2017;318:1550). Protocol: rectal temp 33.0–34.0°C for 72h; do not cool <33°C (cardiac arrhythmia). NNT ~7. EPO + hypothermia = ไม่ช่วย (Wu NEJM 2022) — อย่าเพิ่ม EPO. Control infants ที่ hyperthermic มี worse outcomes — avoid fever in ALL HIE infants.

PEARL 2 — Phenobarbital > Levetiracetam สำหรับ Neonatal Seizures ใน HIE
RCT Sharpe Pediatrics 2020: phenobarbital 80% seizure-free vs levetiracetam 28% (P<.001) — levetiracetam INFERIOR as first-line. Phenobarbital ยังเป็น first choice. Second line: lidocaine add-on (ดีกว่า midazolam ระหว่าง hypothermia) — ห้ามใช้ lidocaine ถ้าให้ phenytoin แล้ว (cardiotoxicity). Pharmacokinetics ของ lidocaine + midazolam เปลี่ยนระหว่าง cooling — ปรับ dose. Clinical seizures recognized <10% of actual electrographic seizures — ต้องใช้ continuous aEEG/EEG.

PEARL 3 — BGT Pattern vs Watershed Pattern: Sentinel vs Prolonged Partial
Acute sentinel event (cord prolapse, uterine rupture) → basal ganglia + thalamus (BGT) + brainstem injury — dyskinetic CP, swallowing/speech difficulties, ↓ IQ. Prolonged partial asphyxia → watershed/parasagittal cortical injury — spastic quadriplegia + cognitive impairment. MRI: PLIC signal loss = strong adverse motor outcome predictor. Placental pathology confirms: chronic villitis → BGT; ↓ placental maturation → watershed. Pattern predicts clinical syndrome → counselling + rehabilitation planning.

PEARL 4 — Mild HIE ≠ Benign: School-Age Sequelae
อย่า reassure ครอบครัวว่า mild HIE = OK. Conway (Early Hum Dev 2018): mild-moderate HIE survivors without major neurologic deficit มี: ↓ full-scale IQ, minor neurologic dysfunction, perceptual-motor difficulties, ADHD requiring extra educational support. Lee-Kelland (2020): แม้แต่ without CP → ↓ cognitive + motor + emotional outcomes vs controls. Mammillary body atrophy on MRI correlates with ↓ IQ + ↓ episodic memory at school age (Annink 2021). ต้อง inform ครอบครัว + setup ND follow-up อย่างน้อยถึงวัยเรียน.

PEARL 5 — ADC Pseudonormalization: MRI Timing Matters
DWI restricted diffusion (↓ ADC) = cytotoxic edema → ช่วงที่ดีที่สุดในการเห็น injury = days 2–5. ADC pseudonormalization เกิดขึ้น ~day 7–10 — ถ้า MRI ช้าเกินไปอาจ underestimate injury (Bednarek Neurology 2012). เป้าหมาย: DWI ก่อน pseudonormalization + conventional MRI days 3–7 + 1H-MRS days 7–14. Lactate peak บน MRS หลัง 48h = poor prognostic marker; NAA ↓ = neuronal loss. Cerebellar injury underestimated by MRI vs histology — check cerebellum on all HIE scans.
13. Evidence Update — Key Studies
Jacobs SE et al Cochrane Database 2013;1:CD003311: cooling for newborns with HIE — updated meta-analysis 8 RCTs; RD −0.14 (95% CI −0.19 to −0.09); NNT ~7; head cooling = whole-body cooling
Wu YW et al N Engl J Med 2022;387:148: EPO + hypothermia vs hypothermia alone — NO reduction in death or ND impairment; EPO not recommended as adjunct to cooling
Sharpe C et al Pediatrics 2020;145:e20036806: levetiracetam vs phenobarbital RCT — phenobarbital 80% seizure-free vs levetiracetam 28%; phenobarbital remains first-line for neonatal seizures
Laptook AR et al JAMA 2017;318:1550: hypothermia initiated after 6h of age — no significant ↓ death or disability; confirms 6h window
Glass HC et al JAMA Neurol 2021;78:817: safety of early discontinuation of ASM after acute symptomatic neonatal seizures — safe to discontinue before discharge; 2-year outcome similar
Shah PS Semin Fetal Neonatal Med 2010;15:238: safety meta-analysis 8 RCTs: ↑ arrhythmia RR 4.08; ↑ thrombocytopenia RR 1.28; no ↑ other adverse events
Lee-Kelland R et al Arch Dis Child FN 2020;105:8: school-age outcomes of children without CP after hypothermia 2008–2010: ↓ cognitive + motor + emotional outcomes vs controls
Annink KV et al Sci Rep 2021;11:5017: mammillary body atrophy + other MRI correlates of school-age outcome after neonatal HIE: ↓ total + performance IQ + processing speed + episodic memory
Parmentier CEJ et al J Pediatr 2022;245:30: hypoglycemia in infants with HIE → additional brain injury + worse ND outcome; avoid hypoglycemia aggressively

Murray DM et al Arch Dis Child FN 2008;93:F187: gap between electrographic seizure burden, clinical expression, and staff recognition of neonatal seizures — <10% identified by clinical staff; continuous EEG/aEEG mandatory
Cowan FM et al Lancet 2003;361:736: origin and timing of brain lesions in term infants with neonatal encephalopathy: majority of damage in immediate perinatal period; not long-standing injury
Shah DK et al Arch Dis Child FN 2014;99:F219: electrographic seizures associated with brain injury in newborns undergoing hypothermia: OR 5.00 (95% CI 1.47–17.05)
Conway JM et al Early Hum Dev 2018;120:80: mild HIE and long-term ND outcome systematic review: ↓ IQ, minor neurologic dysfunction, ADHD even without major disability
Weeke LC et al J Pediatr 2018;192:33: novel MRI score predicting ND outcome after perinatal asphyxia + hypothermia
Maiwald CA et al BMC Pediatr 2019;19:210: ALBINO trial protocol: allopurinol + hypothermia for HI brain injury (phase III RCT, ongoing)
Bednarek N et al Neurology 2012;78:1420: impact of hypothermia on MRI diffusion changes in neonatal encephalopathy — DWI ADC pseudonormalization dynamics; optimal MRI window days 2–5
Chalak L et al J Pediatr 2021;237:190: acute + chronic placental abnormalities in HIE cohort: chronic villitis → BGT pattern; ↓ placental maturation → WM/watershed injury
Lorek A et al Pediatr Res 1994;36:699: secondary cerebral energy failure after acute HI in newborn piglet: continuous 48h 31P-MRS; established timeline of secondary energy failure
14. Self-Check Q&A
Q: อธิบาย two-phase injury ใน HIE: timeline, mechanism, therapeutic window

ตอบ: Primary energy failure: Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase failure → cytotoxic edema → lactic acidosis → primary necrosis if severe. Latent period (30–60 min): apparent recovery of cerebral oxidative metabolism. Secondary energy failure: starts 6–8 hours; mitochondrial failure → ↓ ATP + PCr (31P-MRS); lasts 72h; results in delayed apoptosis/necrosis. Therapeutic window = 6 hours — this is why hypothermia must start within 6h; starting later → significantly less effective (Laptook JAMA 2017).
Q: Sarnat Stage II vs Stage III: อาการหลัก และ outcome ที่ต่างกัน

ตอบ: Stage II (moderate): lethargic; ↓ spontaneous movement; miosis (parasympathetic); bradycardia; ↑ GI motility; weak Moro/suck/grasp; mild hypotonia; strong distal flexion posture. Stage III (severe): stuporous; absent movement; variable autonomic; absent all primitive reflexes; flaccid; intermittent decerebration. Outcomes (pre-hypothermia): moderate = ~75% survive without major deficit; severe = 50–100% mortality + 65–75% severe disability in survivors. With cooling: severity now primarily a selection criterion.
Q: BGT injury pattern vs Watershed injury pattern: insult type, MRI, clinical outcome

ตอบ: BGT — sentinel event: acute total asphyxia (cord prolapse); MRI = BGT signal abnormality + PLIC signal loss; outcome = dyskinetic CP + swallowing/speech difficulties. Watershed/Parasagittal — prolonged partial: 1–5h partial asphyxia; MRI = cortical watershed DWI restriction, parasagittal signal; outcome = spastic quad/diplegia + cognitive impairment. Placental pathology (Chalak 2021): chronic villitis → BGT; ↓ placental maturation → watershed.
Q: Phenobarbital vs Levetiracetam ใน neonatal seizures: ผลของ RCT และ drug interactions ที่ต้องระวัง

ตอบ: Sharpe Pediatrics 2020: phenobarbital 80% seizure-free vs levetiracetam 28% — phenobarbital first-line. Lidocaine as add-on: more effective than midazolam after phenobarbital (Weeke 2016), especially during cooling; DO NOT give lidocaine if phenytoin already given — cardiotoxicity. Pharmacokinetics of lidocaine + midazolam altered by hypothermia → dose adjustment required. Early ASM discontinuation before discharge is safe (Glass JAMA Neurol 2021).
Q: EPO ช่วยอะไรใน HIE ถ้าใช้ร่วมกับ hypothermia?

ตอบ: Wu NEJM 2022;387:148: phase III RCT — EPO + hypothermia does NOT reduce death or neurodevelopmental impairment vs hypothermia alone. ข้อสรุป: ไม่ควรเพิ่ม EPO เป็น adjunct กับ hypothermia. หมายเหตุ: EPO อาจเคย show benefit ในรายที่ไม่ได้รับ hypothermia — แต่เมื่อ combine กับ cooling ไม่ได้ช่วยเพิ่ม. Melatonin, allopurinol (ALBINO trial ongoing), xenon (no benefit demonstrated), stem cells (ongoing) — ยังไม่มีอะไรที่พิสูจน์แล้วเป็น effective adjunct.
Q: MRI ใน HIE: timing ที่ดีที่สุด, ลำดับที่ควรทำ, ADC pseudonormalization คืออะไร

ตอบ: DWI: days 2–5 (optimal) — restricted diffusion = cytotoxic edema; ADC pseudonormalization ~day 7–10 (ADC returns to normal despite injury still present → if MRI late, will underestimate injury; cystic areas later show ↑ ADC above normal). Conventional MRI (T1/T2): days 3–7; PLIC signal loss = strong adverse motor predictor. 1H-MRS: days 7–14; lactate ↑ = poor prognosis; NAA ↓ = neuronal loss.
Q: aEEG ใน HIE: background patterns, limitations ใน seizure detection, timing ของ normalization

ตอบ: Background patterns: (1) Normal continuous voltage = very good outcome; (2) Moderately abnormal (continuous low voltage, burst suppression) = moderate HIE, needs serial; (3) Severely abnormal (isoelectric, burst suppression with long flat periods) = poor prognosis. Limitations: misses short, low-amplitude, and focal seizures; single-channel sensitivity 27–56%; two-channel + raw EEG 76%. Normalization timing: good outcome = normalize by 24h (normothermia) or 48h (cooling); predictive value at 3–6h is 59% with cooling vs 84% normothermia.
Q: Multiorgan involvement ใน HIE: cardiovascular + hematologic complications + management

ตอบ: Cardiovascular: myocardial dysfunction in many infants; tricuspid regurgitation; myocardial ischemia on ECG; hypotension (inotropes > volume); PPHN (especially with sepsis/MAS — iNO + milrinone). Hematologic: DIC common; ↑ D-dimer, fibrin degradation products; low factor XIII; NO systemic heparinization; replace platelets/clotting factors as indicated; hypothermia → ↓ thrombocyte counts (RR 1.28). Glucose: avoid hypoglycemia (Parmentier 2022 — additional brain injury); monitor frequently; avoid high-concentration glucose routine.
Q: School-age outcomes ใน HIE survivors: mild HIE มี sequelae อะไร บ้าง

ตอบ: Mild-moderate HIE without major deficit: ↓ full-scale IQ, minor neurologic dysfunction, perceptual-motor difficulties, ADHD, need for special education (Conway Early Hum Dev 2018). Post-hypothermia era (Lee-Kelland 2020): children without CP still show ↓ cognitive + motor + emotional outcomes vs age/sex/SES controls. Mammillary body atrophy (Annink 2021): ↓ total IQ, ↓ performance IQ, ↓ processing speed, ↓ episodic memory. Conclusion: HIE is NOT binary; ALL moderate-severe HIE children need school-age ND follow-up regardless of early normal exam.
Q: Excitotoxicity cascade: glutamate → … → cell death — intermediate steps

ตอบ: Glutamate ↑ → NMDA receptor activation → Ca²⁺ influx → (1) activate lipases/proteases/endonucleases → membrane breakdown; (2) activate nNOS → NO• → combine with O₂•⁻ → peroxynitrite → lipid peroxidation + mitochondrial nitrosylation; (3) mitochondrial failure → caspase-3 release → DNA fragmentation → apoptosis. Parallel: activated neutrophils + microglia → more free radicals. Free iron → Fenton reaction → ↑ toxic radicals. Key: eNOS (endothelial) PROTECTS cerebral perfusion — only inhibit nNOS/iNOS for neuroprotection.
Q: Hypothermia protocol: temp target, duration, GA criteria, timing, ที่ห้ามทำ

ตอบ: Target: whole-body rectal 33.0–34.0°C for 72h; head-cooling: 10°C coolant, body 34–35°C. GA: ≥35 weeks — not recommended in preterm (historical evidence of excess deaths). Timing: must start within 6h of birth; significantly less effective after 6h. Do NOT: cool <33°C (cardiac arrhythmia); avoid hyperthermia in all HIE infants (control infants who became hyperthermic had worse outcomes). Complications: ↑ arrhythmia (RR 4.08), ↑ thrombocytopenia (RR 1.28); rewarming at 0.5°C/hr.

Day 154 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.57
บทต่อไป: Chapter 57 — Seizures in Neonates. เตรียมตัว: classification of neonatal seizures (subtle, clonic, tonic, myoclonic, epileptic spasm); pathophysiology — ทำไม neonatal brain ถึง more susceptible to seizures; EEG-confirmed vs clinical seizures: electroclinical dissociation, status epilepticus; etiologies (HIE most common 38%, stroke 18%, hemorrhage 11%); treatment algorithm: phenobarbital, lidocaine, levetiracetam, benzodiazepines, topiramate; long-term epilepsy risk + NDO; ACNS guidelines for continuous EEG monitoring in neonates (2011).
